question: 'Individual differences' in organizational behavior refers to:
options: Differences in employee salaries and compensation packages

The variation across people in personality, values, attitudes, cognitive ability, skills, and demographics that affects how they think, behave, and perform at work; understanding individual differences helps managers: assign work that matches individual strengths, customize motivation approaches, predict fit between individuals and roles/cultures, and understand why identical management practices produce different responses from different people
The physical differences in how employees look
Differences in work hours and schedule preferences across employees
explanation: Individual differences and management: the premise of most modern HRM is that individuals aren't interchangeable — a person's personality, values, and cognitive style affect their fit with different roles, teams, and organizational cultures. Selection systems (job interviews, assessments) attempt to predict which individual differences matter for specific roles. The management implication: if you know that an employee has a high need for achievement (McClelland's theory), assign challenging projects with clear metrics; if they have high sociability, give them customer-facing roles. Ignoring individual differences treats everyone as identical — producing misaligned roles and demotivation.

question: The Big Five personality traits (OCEAN) are the most empirically validated framework for personality in organizational settings. Which combination correctly names all five?
options: Intelligence, Motivation, Creativity, Adaptability, Teamwork

Openness to Experience (intellectual curiosity, imagination), Conscientiousness (organized, dependable, goal-directed), Extraversion (sociable, talkative, assertive), Agreeableness (cooperative, trusting, empathic), Neuroticism (emotional instability, anxiety, irritability) — OCEAN; most predictive of job performance: Conscientiousness (highest predictor across almost all roles — organized, reliable people tend to perform better); most predictive of leadership emergence: Extraversion
Dominance, Influence, Steadiness, Conscientiousness — the DISC model
Introversion, Sensing, Thinking, Judging — the Myers-Briggs ISTJ
explanation: Big Five vs. MBTI: the Big Five has significantly stronger empirical support than MBTI for predicting job performance. Key research findings: (1) Conscientiousness predicts job performance across virtually all job types — the most universal predictor; (2) Extraversion predicts performance in sales and leadership roles; (3) Emotional Stability (low Neuroticism) predicts performance in high-stress roles; (4) Openness predicts creative performance and adaptation to change; (5) Agreeableness predicts performance in cooperative/service roles. MBTI (Myers-Briggs): widely used in organizations but has weak test-retest reliability (people get different types on retesting) and weaker predictive validity for job performance compared to Big Five.

question: 'Locus of control' is a personality dimension that affects workplace behavior. An employee with an 'internal locus of control' believes that:
options: Their career outcomes are determined primarily by luck and external factors beyond their control

Their own actions, skills, and effort primarily determine their outcomes — they believe they control their own fate; compared to external locus employees (who attribute outcomes to luck, fate, or other people), internal locus employees: take more initiative, persist longer in the face of obstacles, seek feedback to improve performance, and are more likely to take accountability for results; internal locus employees tend to be better performers in roles requiring autonomy; external locus employees may perform better in highly constrained, role-defined jobs where personal initiative isn't required or valued
Success at work is determined primarily by relationships and networking
The locus of control is a fixed brain structure that determines personality from birth
explanation: Locus of control (Rotter, 1966): the dimension predicts a wide range of behaviors. Internal locus: 'I can influence my situation'; leads to: proactive problem-solving, self-directed learning, resilience, and higher performance in autonomous roles. External locus: 'Things happen to me'; may lead to: passive waiting for direction, lower resilience to setbacks, but may also produce lower stress in situations outside their control (less personal distress about macro factors). Management application: internal locus employees thrive in entrepreneurial, autonomous, project-based work; they prefer feedback and take developmental criticism constructively. External locus employees may perform better in highly structured, procedural roles and may struggle with ambiguity. Selection implications: for roles requiring initiative and self-direction, internal locus is a significant predictor of success.

question: 'Cognitive ability' (often measured as IQ or g-factor intelligence) is one of the strongest predictors of job performance across almost all roles. Why does higher cognitive ability predict better performance even for jobs that don't seem intellectually demanding?
options: Higher IQ employees can work faster at all tasks including physical labor

Higher cognitive ability enables faster learning, more complex information processing, and better problem-solving under novel conditions: even 'simple' jobs involve: learning procedures, adapting to exceptions, diagnosing unusual situations, and solving problems that don't match the training; employees with higher cognitive ability learn job knowledge faster (shorter time to proficiency), handle rare exceptions without escalating (important for cost efficiency), adapt better to process changes, and generate solutions to problems that exceed the standard procedure; the meta-analytic evidence: cognitive ability is the single best predictor of job performance across job families, education levels, and performance criteria — a finding replicated across hundreds of studies in different countries
Cognitive ability only predicts performance in academic or research positions
The relationship between cognitive ability and job performance is too weak to be practically useful
explanation: Cognitive ability and job performance (Schmidt and Hunter, 1998): the most comprehensive meta-analysis of predictors of job performance found cognitive ability had the highest validity coefficient (.51) of any single predictor. The mechanism: g-factor (general intelligence) is essentially the ability to learn, reason, and solve novel problems. Every job involves novel problems — even a 'simple' job like cashier involves: handling angry customers (social problem-solving), resolving register errors (numerical problem-solving), and adapting to new procedures (learning). Higher g = faster adaptation, better exception handling, and more valuable when conditions change. The compounding effect: over time, higher-g employees continue to improve while lower-g employees plateau — the performance gap grows with tenure. Combined with conscientiousness, cognitive ability + conscientiousness is the strongest two-variable prediction of long-term job performance.

question: A company's sales team has two high performers: Alex, who is highly extraverted, relationship-focused, and thrives on customer interaction; Sam, who is introverted, analytically rigorous, and produces the highest-quality technical proposals in the team. Both are being considered for Sales Director. What individual differences dimension should inform the decision?
options: The Sales Director role should always go to the more extraverted candidate

Role requirements vs. individual fit: Sales Director involves: managing and developing a team of salespeople (requires coaching ability, empathy, ability to motivate diverse individuals), representing the sales function to executive leadership (requires organizational navigation, strategic communication), and modeling effective sales behavior (requires the behaviors that make salespeople succeed). If the Sales Director role is primarily about people leadership: Alex's extraversion and relationship skills may be a better fit. If the role requires analytical rigor and technical depth in client proposals: Sam's analytical orientation may be more valuable. The critical error: promoting the top individual performer into management — both Alex and Sam are excellent at INDIVIDUAL sales; neither has demonstrated management ability; the skills required for Sales Director (coaching, organizational navigation, team development) are different from individual sales skills. The Peter Principle risk: both are in danger of being promoted to their level of incompetence
The decision should be based solely on sales revenue metrics from the past 12 months
Introversion always disqualifies a candidate from leadership positions
explanation: Individual differences and role fit: this question illustrates the 'best performer ≠ best manager' problem. Research (Gallup, 2015): only 1 in 10 people have the rare combination of talents required to be an effective manager; most companies promote high performers into management and see both management quality and performance suffer simultaneously (the high performer is less effective in their new role, and their team is less effectively led than it would be with a natural manager). The diagnostic question: 'What specific skills and behaviors does the Sales Director role require?' Then: 'Which candidate has those skills and can develop them further?' Individual sales performance answers a different question. The individual differences insight: introversion vs. extraversion matters less than coaching ability, emotional intelligence, and strategic thinking for most management roles.

question: 'Stereotype threat' (Claude Steele) describes a phenomenon where:
options: Managers make inaccurate assessments because of their own stereotypes

Members of groups associated with negative stereotypes about their ability in a domain perform worse when the stereotype is made salient — not because of the stereotypes' validity, but because awareness of the stereotype creates cognitive burden and anxiety that interferes with performance; the mechanism: a woman told before a math test 'women typically do worse on this test' performs worse than an equally capable woman given no such information; the impairment is mediated by cognitive load (monitoring for confirming the stereotype consumes mental resources); the organizational implication: diverse employees may underperform relative to their true capability when organizational culture makes them aware of stereotypes about their group, even if no individual explicitly discriminates
Employees who know about stereotypes can't be affected by them
Stereotype threat only affects performance on standardized tests, not workplace performance
explanation: Stereotype threat in organizational contexts: the original Steele and Aronson research (1995) showed Black college students performed worse on verbal tests when they were told the test measured intellectual ability (activating stereotype awareness) vs. when framed as a lab task. The effect has been replicated: women in math, older workers on memory tasks, lower SES students on academic tasks. The organizational implications: performance evaluation systems that make demographic group membership salient (through comparison groups, stereotype-activating framing) may suppress performance by exactly the diverse groups companies are trying to support. Creating inclusive environments that don't activate stereotype threat: use growth mindset framing (this measures learning, not fixed ability), normalize diversity in high-performance roles (representation reduces threat for underrepresented groups), and design evaluations that don't prime group identity.

question: Google's People Operations team conducted research on what separates high-performing teams from average teams. The research (Project Aristotle, 2012) found that who is on the team matters less than:
options: The team's average IQ and educational credentials

How the team works together — specifically, the processes and norms the team uses; psychological safety was the most important factor (team members feel safe speaking up, admitting errors, taking risks); other significant factors: dependability (team members can rely on each other to deliver), structure and clarity (goals and roles are clear), meaning (the work is personally meaningful), and impact (the team believes their work matters); high individual talent without these team process factors did not predict high team performance
The size of the team's budget
The seniority of the team's manager
explanation: Project Aristotle's counterintuitive findings: Google's data science team expected to find that the best teams were composed of the smartest or most technically skilled people. Instead: individual characteristics (IQ, education, personality traits individually) were far less predictive than team-level processes. The implication: adding a brilliant engineer to a team with low psychological safety may reduce performance (by intimidating team members into silence). The management intervention: invest in team process — how does the team communicate? How are conflicts handled? Does everyone contribute in meetings? These questions determine whether the team's collective intelligence exceeds (or falls short of) its average individual intelligence. The 'collective intelligence' research (Woolley et al.): team performance is predicted by social sensitivity (reading others' emotions) and equal contribution in conversations, not by average IQ.

question: An employer hires based on 'cultural fit' — selecting candidates who share the same communication style, social preferences, and background as the existing team. The hiring manager argues: 'We need people who will work well with the current team.' What individual differences and organizational risks does this approach create?
options: Cultural fit hiring always produces the best organizational outcomes since team cohesion is critical

Cultural fit hiring risks: (1) Demographic homogeneity — 'similar to us' often translates to same background, university, social class, and demographic, creating implicit bias against underrepresented groups; (2) Cognitive homogeneity — people who think alike miss each other's blind spots; groupthink becomes more likely; problems are analyzed through one lens; (3) Legal risk — in the US, if cultural fit criteria correlate with protected characteristics (race, gender, national origin), they can create disparate impact discrimination; (4) Strategy risk — organizations that succeed in changing competitive environments typically need to add diversity of thought, not replicate past successful individuals; the alternative: 'culture add' or 'values alignment' hiring — assess whether candidates share the organization's fundamental values (customer-focus, honesty, excellence) while welcoming different communication styles, backgrounds, and approaches; this separates values alignment (meaningful) from cultural sameness (problematic)
Cultural fit hiring is always illegal regardless of organizational context
Only the most senior executive roles require cultural fit assessment
explanation: Cultural fit vs. cultural add: 'cultural fit' is one of the most frequently cited hiring criteria in corporate settings, but also one of the most critiqued. The organizational behavior research: high-performing organizations consistently show that the 'people like me' bias in cultural fit hiring creates demographic and cognitive homogeneity that reduces innovation and increases groupthink. Netflix's talent hiring criteria: they explicitly screen for 'cultural add' — do you bring something we don't have? — rather than 'cultural fit' — are you like us? The legal dimension: EEOC (Equal Employment Opportunity Commission) guidance says selection criteria that correlate with protected characteristics require demonstration of business necessity. If 'cultural fit' criteria produce disparate outcomes for protected groups, they must be validated as job-related. The practical solution: define specific, assessable behaviors that predict success in the role, not abstract 'fit.'

question: An employee's job performance has declined significantly over 6 months. Their manager investigates and finds: (1) The employee has the same skills as when they were hired; (2) The employee used to care deeply about the work but says 'I'm just going through the motions'; (3) The employee's personal situation has changed significantly (divorce, child health issue). What individual differences framework helps diagnose this situation?
options: This is a clear performance management issue requiring progressive discipline

Ability × Motivation × Opportunity performance model: performance = f(Ability × Motivation × Opportunity); the investigation already eliminated the ability explanation (skills unchanged); Motivation: the employee's stated disengagement ('going through the motions') points to motivation as the cause; the personal situation (divorce, child health) has shifted psychological resources away from work; this is a temporal individual difference — the person's current motivational state is depleted by non-work stressors; Opportunity: worth investigating — has the role changed? does the employee have the resources and context to succeed? The management response: motivation diagnosis first — is this temporary (personal situation stabilizes → motivation recovers) or longer-term (fundamental mismatch between role and what employee now values)? Responding with progressive discipline (ability-focused or accountability-focused intervention) misdiagnoses a motivation problem as a discipline problem
Personal life situations have no bearing on workplace performance management
The employee should be immediately placed on a performance improvement plan
explanation: The A × M × O framework (Ability × Motivation × Opportunity): different root causes require different management interventions. Ability gap → training, coaching, role adjustment. Motivation gap → engagement diagnosis, role redesign, personal support, career conversation. Opportunity gap → resource provision, obstacle removal, process improvement. Diagnosing the wrong root cause produces the wrong intervention: applying training (ability intervention) to a motivation problem wastes time and frustrates the employee; applying discipline (accountability intervention) to a temporary motivation crisis driven by personal circumstances damages trust and accelerates disengagement. The compassionate management response: acknowledge the personal challenges; offer appropriate resources (EAP, temporary workload adjustment); give the employee agency in designing a performance recovery plan; set clear expectations for recovery timeline and criteria. This addresses the actual root cause rather than the symptom.

question: Research consistently shows that unstructured job interviews (conversational, covering different topics for each candidate) have low predictive validity for job performance. Structured interviews (same questions for all candidates, behavioral or situational questions, standardized scoring) have significantly higher validity. Why?
options: Unstructured interviews are better because they reveal more about the candidate's personality

Standardization removes idiosyncratic interviewer bias and ensures comparable information: unstructured interviews: different candidates are asked different questions (you can't compare answers across candidates); interviewer impressions are influenced by: initial 30-second impression (primacy bias), similarity bias (liking candidates like themselves), halo effect (one impressive answer inflates overall evaluation), and interview fatigue (later candidates scored differently than earlier ones with identical answers); structured interviews: asking identical questions allows direct comparison; behavioral questions ('Tell me about a time when...') assess past behavior in the same domain across all candidates; scoring rubrics reduce idiosyncratic rating variation; interviewers who are calibrated (agreed on what 'good' looks like) produce more reliable evaluations; the evidence: structured interviews have 2× the predictive validity of unstructured interviews for job performance (meta-analysis, Schmidt and Hunter 1998)
Structured interviews reduce candidate comfort and therefore reduce information quality
The type of interview structure has no measurable impact on hiring quality
explanation: Interviewing research: work sample tests (actually performing the job) and structured behavioral interviews are the highest validity predictors of job performance that are practical to administer (cognitive ability tests are the highest overall but raise equity concerns for adverse impact). The typical organizational failure: hiring managers believe their unstructured interview judgment is highly accurate (the 'confidence' of their impressions is high), but research consistently shows unstructured interviewer judgments predict job performance barely better than chance. The mechanism: unstructured interviews measure 'do I like this person?' more than 'can this person do this job?' — interviewers overweight conversational charm and penalize nervousness, both of which are orthogonal to job performance.
